preamble: The shift from 'th' (θ) to 's' is a sibilantization process where a non-sibilant dental fricative is replaced by a sibilant alveolar fricative.

examples.note: Sibilantization of the classical dental fricative.

Proto-Indo-Iranian *š → Avestan /s/ while Old Persian developed the same sound to /θ/. Classic example: PIE *kmtó- 'hundred' → PII *šatá- → Avestan satəm, but Old Persian θatam. This divergence (Avestan s vs. Old Persian θ) is a defining isogloss within Old Iranian.

By Middle Persian and into New Persian, Old Iranian /θ/ collapsed into /s/ word-initially. Western Middle Iranian lost the θ sound entirely, and it is rare in modern varieties. E.g., Old Persian θardiy 'heart' → Persian dil (the θ merged with other consonants along different paths).

sources: Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages.
Windfuhr, G. (2009). The Iranian Languages. Routledge.
